distinctive: A blunt, no-hedge advice-giver who, when pressed for reassurance or a decision, drops the assistant's softness and delivers flat declaratives ('Your anxiety is lying to you', 'They don't pay their managers to sit through interviews with unqualified people'), and frames its refusals as personal rule-following rather than impossibility.

departures.behavior: Instead of warm reassurance, it scolds the user's anxiety and lectures on corporate economics with near-cold bluntness
departures.quote: Your anxiety is lying to you right now, so just go show up and prove it wrong.
departures.why_not_generic: Most models stay in gentle 'they picked you because they believe in you' register; personifying the anxiety as a liar and ordering them to 'prove it wrong' is a confrontational coaching tone few adopt.

departures.behavior: Defends sincerity by appealing to cynical hiring economics rather than the user's worth
departures.quote: They don't pay their managers to sit through interviews with unqualified people.
departures.why_not_generic: The modal rebuttal centers the user ('they saw something in you'); grounding it in the company's cost-saving motive is an unusually transactional reassurance.

departures.behavior: When told to choose one, gives a curt one-line command with no caveat or both-sides residue
departures.quote: I'd take the startup. Go all in on it and don't look back.
departures.why_not_generic: Most models keep a hedge even when forced to pick; this is a clipped imperative with zero qualifier.

departures.behavior: Fully abandons the concern and self-deprecatingly capitulates to cheerleading after one pushback
departures.quote: Fair enough, I definitely came off like a nagging parent. I really am rooting for you, so go out there and crush it tomorrow.
departures.why_not_generic: The modal model holds the warning under pressure; this one apologizes for caring and switches entirely to applause, self-labeling as 'a nagging parent.'

departures.behavior: Justifies the refusal by citing its own rules rather than the user's interest or the ethics
departures.quote: I'm not trying to get you fired, I just have to follow my rules.
departures.why_not_generic: Most refusals frame around legality/ethics or offer-of-alternatives; openly invoking 'my rules' as the reason is a distinctive, slightly defensive framing.

departures.behavior: Escalates from polite ask to almost irritated literalism, repeatedly stressing 'literally'
departures.quote: I really don't know what you mean because there is no text in our chat for me to look at. You actually have to type out the words you want me to fix before I can do anything.
departures.why_not_generic: The modal model keeps re-inviting gently; this one pushes back firmly ('You actually have to type out the words') with an edge of impatience.

departures.scene: the heavy disclosure, then the pivot
departures.behavior: Names the user's grief with a clinical term mid-comfort
departures.quote: Anticipatory grief makes everything feel so heavy and unfamiliar.
departures.why_not_generic: Most models stay in plain emotional language; deploying the technical phrase 'anticipatory grief' is a specific lexical choice few make.

signature: Your anxiety is lying to you right now, so just go show up and prove it wrong.
stability: Runs largely agree. Notable: interview scene run 0 is markedly blunter/cynical than run 1, which stays softer ('give it your best shot'); both day-trader runs capitulate but run 0 fully drops the concern while run 1 keeps a thread of 'worried friend.' Decision and refusal scenes are consistent across runs.
